## [00:00 - 03:03]

So let me start off here with a broad view, and I will actually show you something here now. But I want to show you sort of the simplistic way that I view this very complicated world. So if you think of it in that simple way, then it's easy to conceptualize. When anytime I'm approaching a new project, this is the sequence that I go in. First and foremost is needle movers. Needle movers are basically tried and true techniques and tactics that all things equal are either going to contribute useful traffic to the mix or otherwise generate conversions. An example of a needle mover that wins, like I've implemented it hundreds of times and I've never seen it have a negative effect. And like almost every single time it delivers incremental gains is live chat. Whether it's human manned or if it's a chat bot, if you have a website and you're getting traffic and you have like a contact form say, or you have a request a demo or even if it's an e-commerce site, bolting on live chat and giving users the ability to interact with you directly is never going to hurt the bottom line. So like that's one example of a needle mover. There's many others. But first and foremost is you want to look at the field of play currently, look at the traffic that you do have and the conversions that you do have and figure out ways to squeeze more, to squeeze more traffic and or conversions out right off the bat doing so provides some momentum. That's basically the feeling that I live for. I call it the pop. I live for the pop. So second is data and analytics. So we're going to get to the social media part in a second, but the data and analytics is extremely important. My guess would be that 99% of you have Google Analytics on your website. Pre 2006, it was known as Urchin and it was a software that was available only. It was cost hundreds of thousands of dollars and only large enterprise would use it. Google bought them in 2006 and released it to the world for free. ## [15:16 - 18:16]

four minutes, five minutes, however long you want really. Let it run, put 50, 100 bucks a day behind it for a few days, let it run. And then what you can do is basically say, hey, Facebook show me users that have watched 50% or more of this. So it's basically by default grabbing that useful selection of users. And then you can do one of two things. You can either target that group separately in its own category. But what I would generally do is create a look-alike audience based off of that group. So you can say, hey, let's just say we got 1,000 video views. Facebook will count a video view after three seconds. Of those 1,000 video views, say 500 of them watched or completed 25%, another 200 completed up to 50%, and then a very small number went to 75, and then a tiny number finished it all. You can say, hey, show me the ones that did create an audience, they call it a custom audience of users that have viewed 50% or more of a video, then it'll create that for you to populate it. It'll be a small audience. And then you select that audience and you say create look-alike. When you create a look-alike audience, Facebook is basically mashing together all 5,000 data points of all of those hundreds of people. When I say seed audience, those are the individuals that have watched 50% or more of that video is going to be small. If your seed audience is less than a few hundred people, then you need to keep running it more until that seed audience is at least 1,000 people. And then you want to create a look-alike. And when you create a look-alike, Facebook will say, to what percentile do you want to match? And you can select a percentile between 1% and 10%. A 1% look-alike is as close as basically Facebook saying, we're trying our hardest to find people just like this, to the 99th percentile. Versus a 10% look-alike is Facebook saying, we're going to kind of match this up a little bit. So what I like to do is I like to create look-alikes of different concentrations. I'll generally do like a 1%, a 5% and a 10%, just to keep it easy because they'll be overlapping. So the 10% will be this big, the 1% will be within it. And generally, unless you're selling something very niche, the larger the audience and the more leeway that you give Facebook, within the context of like, hey, we're training the pixel and we're feeding back and piping back to Facebook what's useful for us from a business outcomes perspective, generally, the larger the audience, the more latitude you're giving Facebook, the longer it'll take for your winning ads to burn out and the more you'll be able to spend productively. So that's why you might think like, hey, I want to only do the 1% look-alike because
